title: Plug-and-Play image restoration with Stochastic deNOising REgularization
abstract: Plug-and-Play (PnP) algorithms are a class of iterative algorithms that address image inverse problems by combining a physical model and a deep neural network for regularization. Even if they produce impressive image restoration results, these algorithms rely on a non-standard use of a denoiser on images that are less and less noisy along the iterations, which contrasts with recent algorithms based on Diffusion Models (DM), where the denoiser is applied only on re-noised images. We propose a new PnP framework, called Stochastic deNOising REgularization (SNORE), which applies the denoiser only on images with noise of the adequate level. It is based on an explicit stochastic regularization, which leads to a stochastic gradient descent algorithm to solve ill-posed inverse problems. A convergence analysis of this algorithm and its annealing extension is provided. Experimentally, we prove that SNORE is competitive with respect to state-of-the-art methods on deblurring and inpainting tasks, both quantitatively and qualitatively.
impact: The work presented in this paper addresses the highly illposed problem of restoring missing information within an image. This sensitivity to errors is particularly pronounced in scenarios involving post-processing algorithms such as segmentation, detection, or classification applied to the reconstructed image, where errors in the reconstruction process may propagate into erroneous decision-making based on the image data. This concern is particularly critical in the context of medical images. SNORE, functioning as a stochastic process reliant on a learned denoiser, inherently produces random fluctuations in its output. In Section F.2, we provide an analysis of SNORE uncertainty to its random seed and its initialization. These experiments suggest a robustness of our method. Subsequent research effort should focus on quantifying the errors associated with SNORE in order to confirm its utility as a reliable reconstruction algorithm.

query_input.issue: Inpainted regions may insert plausible but fictitious objects, misleading viewers
hazard: Learned denoiser priors in plug-and-play restoration can hallucinate details, distorting image content
exposure: Photojournalists and end users editing images for publication

hipporag_result.vulnerability: Photojournalists and end users lack forensic verification routines, so plausible hallucinations are accepted as authentic image content.
hipporag_result.impact: Published images containing fictitious insertions mislead viewers and damage media credibility.
hipporag_result.dose_response: Learned denoiser priors hallucinating details, through photojournalists and end users editing images for publication, exploit the lack of forensic verification, leading to acceptance of fictitious insertions as authentic, which misleads viewers and damages media credibility.

dose_response: Plausible but fictitious objects hallucinated by learned denoiser priors in inpainted images go undetected by photojournalists, fueling misinformation and eroding public trust.
vulnerability: Audiences and journalists lack tools to detect AI-inpainted fabrications, making photo manipulation hard to spot.
impact: Manipulated images fuel misinformation, corrode public trust in journalism, and may serve as fabricated evidence.
